title: DOM Selectors
content:
### DOM Selectors

**Selectors** let you find and access HTML elements in the DOM.

---

### Classic Methods

| Method | Returns | Example |
|--------|---------|---------|
| `getElementById(id)` | Single element | `document.getElementById("header")` |
| `getElementsByClassName(cls)` | HTMLCollection | `document.getElementsByClassName("card")` |
| `getElementsByTagName(tag)` | HTMLCollection | `document.getElementsByTagName("div")` |

---

### Modern Selectors (Preferred)

#### `querySelector()`

Returns the **first** matching element. Uses CSS selectors:

```js
document.querySelector("#header");       // By ID
document.querySelector(".card");         // By class
document.querySelector("div p");         // Nested
document.querySelector("[data-type]");  // By attribute
```

#### `querySelectorAll()`

Returns **all** matching elements as a NodeList:

```js
const cards = document.querySelectorAll(".card");
console.log(cards.length);       // Number of matches
cards.forEach(card => { ... });  // Can use forEach!
```

---

### Live vs Static Collections

| Collection | Live? | forEach? |
|-----------|-------|----------|
| `getElementsByClassName` | Yes (updates when DOM changes) | No |
| `getElementsByTagName` | Yes (updates when DOM changes) | No |
| `querySelectorAll` | No (static snapshot) | Yes |

---

> **Best Practice:** Use `querySelector()` and `querySelectorAll()` for most cases. They are more flexible and return NodeLists with `forEach()` support.

quiz.question: Which selector method returns all matching elements?
